content:
# Leviticus Chapter 20 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 70r–70v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_020_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_020_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 106–107

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Say to the sons of Israel: Any man of the sons of Israel or of the sojourners who sojourn in Israel who gives any of his children to Molech shall surely be put to death; the people of the land shall stone him with stones.

**3** I myself will set my face against that man and will cut him off from among his people, because he has given one of his children to Molech, to defile my sanctuary and to profane my holy name.

**4** And if the people of the land at all close their eyes to that man when he gives one of his children to Molech, and do not put him to death,

**5** then I will set my face against that man and against his clan and will cut him off from among his people, him and all who follow him in whoring after Molech.

**6** "And should a person turn to mediums and to necromancers, whoring after them, I will set my face against that person and will cut him off from among his people.

**7** Consecrate yourselves, therefore, and be holy, for I am the LORD your God.

**8** Keep my statutes and do them; I am the LORD who sanctifies you.

**9** "For anyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death; he has cursed his father or his mother — his blood is upon him.

**10** "If a man commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor, both the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death.

**11** If a man lies with his father's wife, he has uncovered his father's nakedness; both of them shall surely be put to death — their blood is upon them.

**12** If a man lies with his daughter-in-law, both of them shall surely be put to death; they have committed a perversion — their blood is upon them.

**13** If a man lies with a male as with a woman, both of them have committed an abomination; they shall surely be put to death — their blood is upon them.

**14** If a man takes a woman and her mother also, it is depravity; he and they shall be burned with fire, that there may be no depravity among you.

**15** If a man lies with an animal, he shall surely be put to death, and you shall kill the animal.

**16** If a woman approaches any animal and lies with it, you shall kill the woman and the animal; they shall surely be put to death — their blood is upon them.

**17** "If a man takes his sister, a daughter of his father or a daughter of his mother, and sees her nakedness, and she sees his nakedness, it is a shameful thing, and they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people. He has uncovered his sister's nakedness; he shall bear his iniquity.

**18** If a man lies with a woman during her menstrual impurity and uncovers her nakedness, he has laid bare her flow, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood. Both of them shall be cut off from among their people.

**19** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister or of your father's sister, for that is to lay bare one's own flesh; they shall bear their iniquity.

**20** If a man lies with his uncle's wife, he has uncovered his uncle's nakedness; they shall bear their sin and shall die childless.

**21** If a man takes his brother's wife, it is impurity; he has uncovered his brother's nakedness — they shall be childless.

**22** "You shall therefore keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them, that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out.

**23** And you shall not walk in the statutes of the nation that I am driving out before you, for they did all these things, and I detested them.

**24** But I have said to you: You shall inherit their land, and I will give it to you to possess, a land flowing with milk and honey. I am the LORD your God, who has separated you from the peoples.

**25** You shall therefore distinguish between the clean animal and the unclean, and between the unclean bird and the clean. You shall not make yourselves detestable by animal or by bird or by anything with which the ground teems, which I have set apart for you to hold unclean.

**26** You shall be holy to me, for I the LORD am holy and have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine.

**27** "A man or a woman who is a medium or a necromancer shall surely be put to death. They shall be stoned with stones — their blood is upon them."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 70r–70v:**
Leviticus 20 occupies the latter portion of folio 70r and the opening of folio 70v in the Leningrad Codex, following immediately upon the sexual prohibition catalogue of chapter 18 and the holiness laws of chapter 19. The chapter functions as the penal counterpart to chapter 18: where chapter 18 catalogued forbidden sexual unions and religious abominations, chapter 20 assigns the penalties. The chapter belongs to the Holiness Code (H source, chs. 17–26) and is closely paralleled with Leviticus 18 in structure, subject matter, and terminology.

The Masoretic text of chapter 20 in B19A is well-preserved with no significant lacunae. The chapter opens with the Molech legislation (vv. 1–5), which occupies a prominent structural position — the most severe cultic violation (child sacrifice to a foreign deity) receives the most extensive divine response, including the divine hostility formula "I will set my face against" (ani ethen et-panai, vv. 3, 5), the communal execution protocol (stoning by the people of the land, v. 2), and the extended karet penalty even when the community fails to execute (v. 5). The chapter then treats necromancy (v. 6), issues a renewed holiness call (vv. 7–8), and proceeds through the sexual penalty catalogue (vv. 9–21), concluding with a theological frame linking separation from the nations to Israel's distinctive holiness (vv. 22–27).

The chapter's structural logic mirrors the legal procedure of a covenant treaty: prohibited conduct (ch. 18) → obligations and holiness standards (ch. 19) → sanctions (ch. 20). The death formula mot yumat (shall surely be put to death) appears with high frequency in this chapter (vv. 2, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 27), marking it as the most penalty-intensive chapter in all of Leviticus. The chapter closes with the full-circle return to the medium/necromancer prohibition, now with the death penalty explicitly assigned (v. 27), matching the opening treatment of Molech worship.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 106–107:**
Leviticus 20 in the Vaticanus spans pages 106 and 107. Page 106 (imaged as `LEV_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`) carries the chapter from the Molech legislation through the sexual penalty section, while page 107 (imaged as `LEV_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`) continues through the holiness-separation closing and the medium/necromancer death penalty. The LXX of chapter 20 follows the MT very closely in content and order, with several significant lexical and interpretive variants noted below. The LXX renders the divine hostility formula "I will set my face against" (ani ethen et-panai) as ἐπιστήσω τὸ πρόσωπόν μου ἐπὶ τὸν ἄνθρωπον ἐκεῖνον ("I will set my face upon that man"), a literal structural equivalent. The LXX rendering of havdalah terminology (separation: vv. 24, 25, 26) is particularly significant and is discussed below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "Shall surely be put to death" — mot yumat:**
The formula mot yumat (מוֹת יוּמָת) is the Hiphil imperfect of muth ("to die") with the Qal infinitive absolute as intensifier — literally "dying he shall be caused to die," i.e., "he shall surely be put to death." This construction is the standard legal death-sentence formula in the Pentateuch. It appears nine times in chapter 20 alone (vv. 2, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 27), making it the most death-formula-dense chapter in Leviticus. The rendering "shall surely be put to death" reproduces the emphatic infinitive absolute construction, the active causative sense (someone causes the death — communal execution), and the formulaic legal character. The alternative "must be put to death" (NIV) is more colloquial; "shall be put to death" without "surely" loses the emphatic infinitive absolute. LOGOS preserves "shall surely be put to death" throughout.

**2. "The people of the land shall stone him with stones" (v. 2) — ragom yirgemu oto va-aven am ha-aretz:**
The execution protocol in v. 2b is precise: ragom yirgemu oto ("stoning they shall stone him" — again an infinitive absolute + finite verb), va-aven ("with stones/with a stone"), am ha-aretz ("the people of the land"). The infinitive absolute ragom (from ragam, "to stone") intensifies the finite verb yirgemu — rendered "shall stone him with stones" to preserve both the plurality (aven = "stones," often plural in execution contexts) and the communal nature. Am ha-aretz ("people of the land") here denotes the local community, not a class distinction. The communal stoning protocol underscores that the entire community bears responsibility for executing those who violate the covenant foundations — it is not delegated solely to a judicial officer.

**3. "I myself will set my face against" (v. 3) — ani ethen et-panai:**
The divine hostility formula ani ethen et-panai (lit. "I will give/set my face against") appears three times in this chapter (vv. 3, 5, 6). The pronoun ani ("I") is emphatic in Hebrew — it stands separately before the verb, giving a strong contrastive or emphatic sense: "I — I myself — will set my face against." The rendering "I myself will set my face against" (v. 3) captures this emphasis; in vv. 5 and 6, the less emphatic "I will set my face against" follows without the "myself," as the ani is present but the initial occurrence bore the full rhetorical weight. The idiom "to set one's face against" (natan panim b-) is a strong expression of divine hostility, opposition, and punitive intent — the opposite of "making one's face shine upon" (as in the Aaronic blessing, Num 6:25, "may the LORD make his face shine upon you"). When YHWH sets his face against someone, blessing is withdrawn and judgment is directed.

**4. "Whoring after" (vv. 5, 6) — liznot acharei:**
The verb zanah (Qal, "to commit fornication, whoredom, prostitution") is used metaphorically for idolatry throughout the Holiness Code and the prophets. The construction liznot acharei ("to whore after") — following after Molech (v. 5) or after mediums (v. 6) — treats apostasy as sexual unfaithfulness to YHWH. The rendering "whoring after" preserves the force of this metaphor rather than softening to "going after" or "following." The prostitution imagery for idolatry is a sustained biblical metaphor (Hos 1–3; Ezek 16, 23; Jer 3) and should not be euphemized in formal translation. The rendering "following him in whoring after Molech" (v. 5) and "whoring after them" (v. 6) maintains the visceral language of covenant betrayal.

**5. "Sanctifies you" (v. 8) — mekadeshkhem:**
Verse 8 ends with a participial self-identification formula: YHWH mekadeshkhem — "the LORD who sanctifies you" (Piel participle of qadash, "to make holy, to sanctify, to consecrate"). This formula (and its near-twin in 21:8, 15, 23; 22:9, 16, 32) identifies YHWH as the active agent of Israel's sanctification. The Piel of qadash is causative-declarative: YHWH does not merely command holiness but effects it — he is the one who makes holy. The rendering "I am the LORD who sanctifies you" (rather than "who makes you holy" or "who sets you apart") retains the technical theological term. This participial formula anchors the commands of vv. 7–8 in the divine initiative: Israel is to consecrate itself (v. 7: hitqadashtem — Hithpael, reflexive, "consecrate yourselves") because YHWH is himself making them holy (mekadeshkhem).

**6. "His blood is upon him" — damo bo:**
The phrase damo bo (lit. "his blood is upon him") or dam-hem bam ("their blood is upon them") is the legal responsibility formula for execution: the blood-guilt for the condemned person's death falls on the condemned themselves, not on those who execute them. This formula (vv. 9, 11, 12, 13, 16, 27) releases the executioners from blood-guilt: they are not murderers, because the condemned person's own actions have made their blood their own responsibility. "Their blood is upon them" is the standard formal rendering (ESV, NASB, KJV). The formula is a forensic declaration, not merely a statement of consequence — it is an official legal pronouncement removing liability from the community.

**7. "Perversion" (v. 12) — tevel:**
The noun tevel (occurring only here and in Lev 18:23) is rendered "perversion." The Hebrew root is uncertain; tevel may derive from the root balal ("to mix, confuse, mingle") and refers to a confusion or violation of natural/created order. The LXX renders it as ἀσέβημα ("impious act, impiety"), which captures the religious dimension but not the structural sense. "Perversion" (ESV, NASB) captures both: it denotes an act that perverts the proper order of things. In context (v. 12, man with daughter-in-law), the tevel concept emphasizes that the violation is not merely a social infraction but a corruption of the structuring categories by which relationships are ordered.

**8. "Abomination" (v. 13) — to'evah:**
The term to'evah (תּוֹעֵבָה), rendered "abomination," occurs in v. 13: "both of them have committed an abomination." This is one of the strongest terms in the Hebrew moral lexicon, denoting that which is utterly detestable, repugnant, and incompatible with the divine order. To'evah is applied throughout Leviticus 18 and 20 to various sexual violations, but in the wider OT it covers idolatry (Deut 7:25–26; 17:1), false weights (Prov 11:1), wickedness (Prov 15:9), and much else. The LXX renders to'evah consistently as βδέλυγμα ("abomination, detestable thing") — the word used in the NT at Matt 24:15 (βδέλυγμα τῆς ἐρημώσεως, "abomination of desolation," citing Dan 12:11). The rendering "abomination" is the standard formal-equivalent term and preserves the weight of the Hebrew.

**9. "Depravity" (v. 14) — zimmah:**
The term zimmah (זִמָּה) reappears in v. 14, as in Lev 18:17 and 19:29. It denotes deliberate moral wickedness, a planned or intentional depravity — often rendered "wickedness" (NASB) or "depravity" (ESV alternative). The context (a man marrying both a woman and her mother) identifies the act as zimmah, and the penalty — burning with fire rather than stoning — is unique in the chapter, likely reflecting the compound nature of the violation (involving multiple victims/parties). "Depravity" is preferred over "wickedness" as it better captures the specifically moral-corruption dimension of zimmah as distinct from general sin terms. The purpose clause "that there may be no depravity among you" makes the communal purity rationale explicit.

**10. "Shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people" (v. 17) — ve-nikhretu le-einei bney ammam:**
The karet formula in v. 17 takes an unusual form: ve-nikhretu le-einei bney ammam — "they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people." The standard karet formula is simply "shall be cut off from his/their people" (nikhrat me-ammo); this variant adds le-einei ("before the eyes of, in the sight of"), suggesting a public, visible cutting off — perhaps a formal communal expulsion or shaming, rather than a purely divine act. The rendering "shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people" preserves this distinctive formula verbatim. The unusual character of this variant is noted by the Talmud and commentators as potentially indicating a publicly administered punishment (rather than purely the divine karet).

**11. "It is a shameful thing" (v. 17) — chesed hu:**
The word chesed in v. 17 is the same root as the covenant-faithfulness term translated "covenant-faithfulness" elsewhere (hesed), but here it is used in an archaic or ironic sense meaning "disgrace, shame, impropriety" — a legal technical term for an act that violates social norms and brings shame on the parties. This is one of only two places in the OT where chesed means "shame" rather than "covenant-faithfulness/lovingkindness" (the other is Prov 14:34, where some read yacheret amim — "sin is a reproach to peoples"). The rendering "it is a shameful thing" (ESV: "it is a disgrace") captures this archaic negative sense while distinguishing it from the usual positive sense of hesed. The LXX renders it ἄτοπόν ἐστιν ("it is improper/out of place"), confirming the pejorative reading.

**12. "Died childless" / "shall be childless" (vv. 20–21) — ariri yamut / arurim yihyu:**
Verses 20–21 assign a non-capital penalty to two incestuous unions (uncle's wife, v. 20; brother's wife, v. 21): the parties "shall bear their sin and shall die childless" (v. 20: ariri yamut — "he shall die stripped/childless"; literally ariri may mean "stripped bare" but is consistently understood as "childless, without offspring") and "they shall be childless" (v. 21: arurim yihyu — a different form, arurim, sometimes rendered "childless" but literally may mean "naked/stripped"). The penalty of childlessness (rather than death) in these cases is notable: it is not capital punishment but a covenantal curse — the failure of the lineage, the denial of posterity. "Shall die childless" (v. 20) and "shall be childless" (v. 21) follow the consensus rendering (ESV, NASB) and preserve the distinction between the two forms (yamut = finite verb indicating death in the condition of childlessness; yihyu = they shall be/remain childless).

**13. "Vomit you out" (v. 22) — ve-lo-taqiy et-kem ha-aretz:**
The land-vomiting imagery of Lev 18:25, 28 recurs in 20:22: "that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out." The verb qy (Qal imperfect, "to vomit, spew out") applied to the land ejecting its inhabitants is one of the most vivid and physically arresting metaphors in the Pentateuch. The land is portrayed as a living entity with moral sensibilities that can be nauseated to the point of rejecting its inhabitants. The rendering "vomit you out" preserves this visceral image over softer alternatives ("reject you," "expel you"). The theological force is significant: the land's "vomiting" is not arbitrary — it is provoked by the same moral/religious abominations that preceded Israel's arrival (v. 23: the nations that YHWH is driving out did all these things).

**14. "A land flowing with milk and honey" (v. 24) — eretz zavat chalav u-devash:**
The classic promised-land formula eretz zavat chalav u-devash ("a land flowing/dripping with milk and honey") appears here in v. 24 in the context of Israel's separation from the nations. Its appearance in chapter 20 is not incidental: the land promise and the holiness commands are structurally linked — the same God who separates Israel from the nations also gives them the land. The formula occurs approximately 20 times in the Pentateuch, beginning with Exod 3:8. Chalav (milk) and devash (honey — wild honey or date syrup) together denote a land of abundance and sweetness. The rendering "a land flowing with milk and honey" is the universally standard English form (KJV through ESV).

**15. "Separated you from the peoples" / "I have separated you" (vv. 24, 25, 26) — havdalah terminology:**
The root badal (to separate, distinguish, divide) and its cognates — havdaltikhem min-ha-ammim ("I have separated you from the peoples," v. 24), ve-hivdaltem ben-ha-behemah ha-tehorah la-teme'ah ("you shall distinguish between the clean animal and the unclean," v. 25), va-avdil etkhem min-ha-ammim ("and I have separated you from the peoples," v. 26) — form the theological climax of the chapter. The root badal is the source of the Hebrew concept of havdalah (separation, distinction), which becomes a central organizing category of priestly theology and later Jewish practice (the Havdalah ceremony at the end of Shabbat deliberately invokes this same language from Leviticus).

The three uses of badal in vv. 24–26 create a theological argument:
- YHWH has separated Israel from the nations (v. 24) — a historical-elective act
- Therefore Israel must distinguish between clean and unclean (v. 25) — an applied practical extension
- YHWH has separated Israel to be his own (v. 26) — the grounding purpose

"Separated" is the consistent rendering for Hiphil badal throughout, following ESV/NASB convention and preserving the terminological unity of the passage. The rendering "distinguish" for the medial use (v. 25: ve-hivdaltem) captures the applied sense of making distinctions in the food laws. The theological point is profound: Israel's dietary distinctions and its national distinctiveness are not separate categories — they are the same reality at different scales.

**16. "Detestable" (v. 25) — lo teshaqtsu:**
The verb shaqats (Pi'el, "to make detestable, to detest, to regard as abominable/swarming-creature-impure") in v. 25: "you shall not make yourselves detestable by animal or by bird." The Pi'el form (teshaqtsu, "you shall make detestable" — reflexively, "you shall make yourselves detestable/abominable") draws on the sheqets ("detestable/swarming thing") terminology of Leviticus 11. The rendering "make yourselves detestable" (ESV) captures the reflexive self-contamination sense: eating prohibited animals makes the eater himself into something shequts-like — a violation of the very distinctions God has drawn.

**17. "That you should be mine" (v. 26) — lihyot li:**
Verse 26 closes with lihyot li — "to be mine, to belong to me." The final phrase is compact and intimate: YHWH has separated Israel "to be mine." The lihyot li construction (lit. "for being to me") expresses possession and belonging in its most fundamental covenant form. The LXX renders this ἵνα ἦτε ἐμοί ("that you might be mine/belong to me"), a direct structural equivalent. The rendering "that you should be mine" (rather than "to be mine" — equally valid) carries the purposive nuance of the infinitive and the possessive intimacy of li. This is one of the most concentrated expressions of the covenant relationship in Leviticus: holiness, separation, and belonging to YHWH are one and the same thing.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|------|
| 2 | ve-ha-ger ha-gar be-Yisra'el ("the sojourner who sojourns in Israel") | καὶ ἀπὸ τῶν υἱῶν τῶν προσηλύτων τῶν προσκειμένων ἐν Ἰσραήλ | LXX expands: "and from the sons of the proselytes who are attached among Israel"; MT has simply "sojourners who sojourn"; LXX adds "sons of" and uses προσήλυτος (proselyte) rather than the more neutral πάροικος (sojourner) — a slight interpretive elevation of the legal status |
| 2 | am ha-aretz ("people of the land") | ἡ συναγωγὴ τοῦ Ἰσραήλ ("the congregation of Israel") | LXX replaces "people of the land" with "congregation of Israel" — a significant interpretive move that makes the executing body the formal cultic assembly rather than the local population |
| 3 | ani ethen et-panai ba-ish ha-hu | ἐπιστήσω τὸ πρόσωπόν μου ἐπὶ τὸν ἄνθρωπον ἐκεῖνον | LXX: ἐπιστήσω ("I will set/direct") for natan ("give"); functionally equivalent; πρόσωπον for panim ("face") as standard |
| 9 | damo bo ("his blood is upon him") | ἡ ἐνοχὴ αὐτοῦ ἐστίν ("his guilt is upon him") | LXX: ἐνοχή ("guilt, liability") rather than blood-language; the LXX partially desanguinizes the legal formula — "his guilt is on him" rather than "his blood is on him" |
| 10 | ha-no'ef ve-ha-no'afet ("the adulterer and the adulteress") | ὁ μοιχεύων καὶ ἡ μοιχευομένη | LXX: standard equivalents; μοιχεύω for na'af; MT uses the same root in masculine and feminine forms, LXX active/passive participle pairing |
| 12 | tevel hu ("it is a perversion") | ἀσέβημά ἐστιν ("it is an impious act") | LXX: ἀσέβημα (impiety, sacrilege) rather than a term for confusion-of-categories; the LXX gives a more theological/religious coloring to what the MT frames structurally |
| 13 | to'evah asu shneihem ("both of them have committed an abomination") | βδέλυγμα εἰργάσαντο ἀμφότεροι | LXX: βδέλυγμα for to'evah; standard equivalent; εἰργάσαντο ("worked, perpetrated") for asu ("did") |
| 14 | zimmah hi ("it is depravity") | ἀσέβεια ἐστίν ("it is impiety") | LXX renders zimmah as ἀσέβεια again — a broader term covering godlessness and impiety; the LXX consistently theologizes where the MT uses more specific moral-corruption terms |
| 17 | chesed hu ("it is a shameful thing") | ἄτοπόν ἐστιν ("it is improper/out of place") | LXX: ἄτοπος = "out of place, improper, strange" — captures the archaic pejorative sense of chesed here; confirms that the LXX translators understood this as a negative term, not the normal positive hesed |
| 17 | ve-nikhretu le-einei bney ammam ("they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people") | ἐξολεθρευθήσονται ἐναντίον υἱῶν γένους αὐτῶν | LXX: ἐξολεθρεύω ("destroy utterly, exterminate") rather than the simple "cut off"; a stronger term; ἐναντίον = "before, in the presence of" |
| 20 | ariri yamut ("he shall die childless") | ἄτεκνοι ἀποθανοῦνται ("they shall die childless") | LXX: ἄτεκνος ("childless, without children") — a precise semantic equivalent; MT ariri is literally "stripped bare" but contextually = childless; LXX interpretive rendering confirms the childlessness reading |
| 21 | arurim yihyu ("they shall be childless") | ἄτεκνοι τελευτήσουσιν ("they shall end/die childless") | LXX: again ἄτεκνος; τελευτάω = "to end, to die" — slightly softer than ἀποθανοῦνται in v. 20 |
| 24 | eretz zavat chalav u-devash ("a land flowing with milk and honey") | γῆν ῥέουσαν γάλα καὶ μέλι ("a land flowing with milk and honey") | LXX: standard equivalent; ῥέω ("to flow") for zub ("to flow, ooze") |
| 24 | va-avdil etkhem min-ha-ammim ("I have separated you from the peoples") | ὃς διώρισα ὑμᾶς ἀπὸ πάντων τῶν ἐθνῶν | LXX: διορίζω ("to separate, mark off by a boundary") for badal; ἐθνῶν (nations/Gentiles) for ammim (peoples) — "Gentiles" has stronger theological weight in LXX context |
| 25 | ve-hivdaltem ben-ha-behemah ha-tehorah la-teme'ah ("you shall distinguish between the clean animal and the unclean") | καὶ ἀφοριεῖτε ἀνὰ μέσον τῶν κτηνῶν τῶν καθαρῶν καὶ τῶν ἀκαθάρτων | LXX: ἀφορίζω ("to set apart, separate out") for badal; the LXX uses two different Greek verbs (διορίζω in v. 24, ἀφορίζω in v. 25) for the same Hebrew root — a slight differentiation |
| 26 | ve-avdil etkhem min-ha-ammim lihyot li ("I have separated you from the peoples to be mine") | καὶ διέστειλα ὑμᾶς ἀπὸ πάντων τῶν ἐθνῶν εἶναί μου | LXX: yet another Greek verb for badal — διαστέλλω ("to set apart, distinguish, order strictly"); the three consecutive uses of badal in vv. 24–26 are rendered with three different Greek verbs, showing the LXX translators' sensitivity to context while losing the terminological unity of the MT |
| 27 | ov o yidde'oni ("a medium or a necromancer") | ὃς ἐὰν γένηται ἐγγαστρίμυθος ἢ ἐπαοιδός ("whoever becomes a ventriloquist/belly-speaker or an enchanter") | LXX: ἐγγαστρίμυθος for ov (as in 19:31); ἐπαοιδός ("enchanter, one who speaks spells") for yidde'oni — a different rendering than 19:31 where LXX used τερατοσκόπος; the variation may reflect different LXX traditions for the two chapters |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings

| Hebrew Term | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|-------------|-----------------|-------|
| YHWH | the LORD | Small capitals; standard throughout |
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Formal throughout |
| mot yumat | shall surely be put to death | Infinitive absolute + Hiphil finite; emphatic death formula; 9 occurrences |
| damo bo / dam-hem bam | his/their blood is upon him/them | Legal responsibility formula; releases executioners from blood-guilt |
| Molech | Molech | Preserved; Ammonite deity to whom children were passed through fire |
| ani ethen et-panai | I will set my face against | Divine hostility formula; vv. 3, 5, 6 |
| karet (ve-nikhreti) | cut off / shall be cut off | Divine excision from the covenant community |
| ragom yirgemu | shall stone him with stones | Infinitive absolute + finite; emphatic stoning formula |
| am ha-aretz | people of the land | Local community as executing body |
| zanah (liznot acharei) | whoring after | Metaphor for idolatry/apostasy; preserved, not softened |
| mekadeshkhem | who sanctifies you | v. 8: Piel participle; YHWH as the active agent of sanctification |
| tevel | perversion | v. 12; violation of created order/categories |
| to'evah | abomination | v. 13; strongest moral-condemnation term |
| zimmah | depravity | vv. 14, (cf. 18:17; 19:29); deliberate moral corruption |
| ariri / arurim | childless | vv. 20–21; penalty of dying without offspring |
| qy (vomit) | vomit | v. 22; land ejecting its inhabitants; preserved as vivid metaphor |
| eretz zavat chalav u-devash | a land flowing with milk and honey | v. 24; standard formula; preserved verbatim |
| badal (Hiphil) | separated / distinguish | vv. 24–26; havdalah root; three occurrences unified by single rendering family |
| ov | medium | v. 27; chthonic spirit practitioner |
| yidde'oni | necromancer | v. 27; one with knowledge from the dead |
| chesed (pejorative) | shameful thing | v. 17; archaic negative sense; contrast with covenant-faithfulness usage |
| lihyot li | to be mine | v. 26; covenant possession formula |
| havdaltikhem / va-avdil | I have separated you | vv. 24, 26; divine election-separation act |

---

— LOGOS
